transcript.content:
You are a news summarization assistant. Summarize the following article and extract its key facts. Respond ONLY with a valid JSON object — no prose, no markdown fences:
{
  "summary": "string (150-200 words)",
  "key_facts": ["string","string","string","string","string"],
  "sources_represented": ["string"]
}
Rules:
- summary must be 150-200 words
- key_facts must contain EXACTLY 5 items
- sources_represented must list every distinct perspective included

Summarize this article:

---
HEADLINE: By Ellie CookSenior Defense ReporterShareNewsweek is a Trust Project memberU.S
SOURCE: Newsweek

ARTICLE:

By Ellie CookSenior Defense ReporterShareNewsweek is a Trust Project memberU.S. President Donald Trump has said the U.S. is "seeking immediate negotiations" to acquire Greenland, deepening the yawning chasm opening up between Washington and its closest allies over the Arctic territory.Trump called Greenland "part of North America" and "our territory" during a rambling speech to world leaders at the World Economic Forum in the Swiss town of Davos on Wednesday.The Republican reiterated his claim that the U.S. is the only power that can protect the mostly ice-covered expanse, and insisted this would not pose a threat to NATO.Alliance officials have repeatedly said a U.S. military attack on Greenland, a semi-autonomous part of Denmark, would spell the end of NATO. The prospect of the U.S. — the most influential and powerful member of NATO —attacking territory belonging to a fellow alliance member has always been unfathomable, until now.The White House had refused to rule out using military force to take Greenland, which would be a violation of international law."I don't want to use force. I won't use force," Trump said. "All the United States is asking for is a place called Greenland."This is a breaking story. More to follow....Request Reprint & LicensingSubmit CorrectionView Editorial & AI GuidelinesAdd Newsweek as a preferred source on Google to see more of our trusted coverage when you search.Related PodcastsTop StoriesFor MembersWorldWhy the US Won't Be Coming to Its Longtime Ally's Aid in Syria10 min readFor MembersNewsUncommon Knowledge: China Complaining About Trump's Land Grab? That's Rich5 min readNewsAnti-ICE Resistance Raises Prospect of Government Shutdown6 min readFor MembersOpinionDonald Trump Finally Makes the Right Move on Greenland: 'Immediate Negotiations'5 min readNewsPam Bondi Announces Departure of Lindsey Halligan From DOJ3 min readPoliticsMaryland's New Congressional Map Could Wipe Republicans Out4 min readTrendingDon BaconRepublican Blasts 'Immoral' Trump Actions After Issuing Impeachment Warning7 min readViralShopper Finds $4.99 Sweater in Thrift Store—Then Realizes Royal Connection3 min readDonald TrumpDonald Trump Leaks Private Texts From NATO Allies: Read in Full6 min read25th AmendmentDemocrats Call to Invoke 25th Amendment Against Donald Trump6 min readNcaaCarson Beck Faces Backlash for Postgame Decision After Miami's Loss to Indiana2 min readOpinionOpinionShould the US Put Boots on the Ground in Iran? Newsweek Contributors Debate6 min readOpinionSyria's Kurds Lose Out | Opinion6 min readOpinionAgriculture Secretary: A Year of Results—How the Trump Admin Put American Farmers First | Opinion6 min readOpinionThe New American ICE Age | Opinion5 min readFor MembersOpinionConventional Wisdom: Greenland Edition3 min read
---

```json
{
  "summary": "President Donald Trump announced at the World Economic Forum in Davos that the U.S. is pursuing immediate negotiations to acquire Greenland, escalating tensions with NATO allies. Trump described Greenland as part of North America and claimed the U.S. is the only nation capable of protecting the territory. While stating he does not want to use military force, Trump previously refused to rule out this option entirely. His remarks have alarmed NATO officials, who have warned that any U.S. military action against Greenland—a semi-autonomous territory of Denmark—would effectively end the alliance. The prospect of the U.S., NATO's most powerful member, attacking another member's territory represents an unprecedented challenge to the alliance. Trump insisted his intentions would not threaten NATO, though alliance officials remain deeply concerned about the implications for transatlantic relations and international law.",
  "key_facts": [
    "Trump declared at Davos that the U.S. is seeking immediate negotiations to acquire Greenland, calling it part of North America",
    "The White House previously refused to rule out using military force to take Greenland, though Trump now says he won't use force",
    "NATO officials have warned that a U.S. military attack on Greenland would end the alliance",
    "Greenland is a semi-autonomous territory of Denmark, a NATO member, making Trump's claims unprecedented in alliance history",
    "Any military action to seize Greenland would constitute a violation of international law"
  ],
  "sources_represented": [
    "Newsweek reporting",
    "Donald Trump statements from World Economic Forum",
    "NATO officials",
    "White House position"
  ]
}
```

parsed_output.summary: President Donald Trump announced at the World Economic Forum in Davos that the U.S. is pursuing immediate negotiations to acquire Greenland, escalating tensions with NATO allies. Trump described Greenland as part of North America and claimed the U.S. is the only nation capable of protecting the territory. While stating he does not want to use military force, Trump previously refused to rule out this option entirely. His remarks have alarmed NATO officials, who have warned that any U.S. military action against Greenland—a semi-autonomous territory of Denmark—would effectively end the alliance. The prospect of the U.S., NATO's most powerful member, attacking another member's territory represents an unprecedented challenge to the alliance. Trump insisted his intentions would not threaten NATO, though alliance officials remain deeply concerned about the implications for transatlantic relations and international law.
key_facts: Trump declared at Davos that the U.S. is seeking immediate negotiations to acquire Greenland, calling it part of North America
The White House previously refused to rule out using military force to take Greenland, though Trump now says he won't use force
NATO officials have warned that a U.S. military attack on Greenland would end the alliance
Greenland is a semi-autonomous territory of Denmark, a NATO member, making Trump's claims unprecedented in alliance history
Any military action to seize Greenland would constitute a violation of international law
sources_represented: Donald Trump statements from World Economic Forum
